coredata.dc:title: Comprehensive understanding of biomedical usages of metal and non metal doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Mathew, Anumol
Varghese, Arun
Devi K.R., Sunaja
Pinheiro, Dephan
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, carbon dots have garnered significant attention, particularly within the biomedical realm, owing to their exceptional characteristics. The unique attributes of carbon dots can be further enhanced through the introduction of heteroatom via doping. Various techniques have been devised by researchers to facilitate the doping of carbon dots, with both metallic and non-metallic elements. Elements such as nitrogen (81%), sulfur (67%), and silicon (64.1%) have been successfully employed for doping carbon dots leading to heightened quantum yields. This review compiles the diverse methodologies and elements employed in doping of carbon dots, and their applications in the biomedical domain in recent times. This review discusses the uses of doped carbon dots, both metal and non-metal-doped variants, elucidating their manifold utilities in various biomedical sectors, notably bioimaging, wound healing, and cancer therapy. The discussion culminates by addressing present challenges and offering insights into future prospects of doped carbon dots.

head.ce:title: Comprehensive understanding of biomedical usages of metal and non metal doped carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, carbon dots have garnered significant attention, particularly within the biomedical realm, owing to their exceptional characteristics. The unique attributes of carbon dots can be further enhanced through the introduction of heteroatom via doping. Various techniques have been devised by researchers to facilitate the doping of carbon dots, with both metallic and non-metallic elements. Elements such as nitrogen (81%), sulfur (67%), and silicon (64.1%) have been successfully employed for doping carbon dots leading to heightened quantum yields. This review compiles the diverse methodologies and elements employed in doping of carbon dots, and their applications in the biomedical domain in recent times. This review discusses the uses of doped carbon dots, both metal and non-metal-doped variants, elucidating their manifold utilities in various biomedical sectors, notably bioimaging, wound healing, and cancer therapy. The discussion culminates by addressing present challenges and offering insights into future prospects of doped carbon dots.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are tiny fluorescent carbon based nanoparticles having a sizes of less than 10 nm and have received increasing attention in the last few years [1]. They were discovered accidentally by Xu et al. in 2004 during the course of purifying single-walled carbon nanotubes. The salient features of CDs that make it an attractive material are, great biocompatibility, good photoluminescence, small size, water solubility, low toxicity, good photo/chemical stability, high quantum yield (QY), ease of fabrication, low cost, high surface functionalization, and good anti-photobleaching property. These properties enable significant potential in a range of applications across diverse fields, particularly within the realm of biomedicine. The possible applications in the biomedical field include bioimaging, biosensing, drug delivery, tissue engineering, cancer therapy, wound healing, antibacterial, and drug delivery [1–5]. Significant benefits of carbon dots with sizes smaller than 10 nm include good biocompatibility, distinctive mechanical/thermal features, and simple and multi-functionality capabilities [6–8]. When compared to typical fluorescent dyes, CDs with strong fluorescence and distinctive photoluminescence features show superior fluorescence properties, such as resistance to photo-bleaching, photostability, and are non-blinking [6,9]. Surface functionalization of CDs can significantly improve their properties, making them efficient candidates for use in the domains of bio and nanomedicine.

Generally, CDs have a spherical or quasi spherical shape and are nanocrystalline in nature [10–12]. The core of CDs is composed of an sp² conjugated nanocrystalline carbon system that is embedded inside an sp³ carbon matrix which is amorphous and has surface defects resulting from surface oxidation during the synthesis process [13]. A carbon core and surface groups containing oxygen or nitrogen make up the structure of a single CD [14]. Top-down or bottom-up methods are followed for the synthesis of CDs, of which the bottom-up approach suits large-scale synthesis of high fluorescent CDs [3]. Different methods have been developed to fabricate CDs like electrochemical [15], hydrothermal, arc discharge, microwave, chemical oxidation, laser ablation, acidic oxidation, thermal/combustion, and pyrolysis [16–23]. The good repeatability of the product makes hydrothermal process the most widely used method [24]. Due to the high atom economy and facile instrumentation process, hydrothermal technique is considered the more convenient and effective method for synthesizing CDs, as no post-surface passivation is required for this [25]. D. Pan et al. first reported the hydrothermal route for the synthesis of CDs [26]. For synthesizing CDs having high photoluminescent QY, the hydrothermal and solvothermal methods are preferred, besides being ideal for synthesizing CDs on a large scale [27–29].

When compared to quantum dots, CDs have some drawbacks, including poor QY and focused luminosity in the blue to green region [30]. Scientists have put a lot of work into enhancing the luminescence of CDs, including doping with heteroatoms and expanding the conjugated structure [31–33]. Through surface modifications and heteroatom doping, CDs can achieve multi-functionality, which is a suitable method for broadening and improving their applications and performance [34,35]. The electronic and optical properties of CDs can be modified after doping with different metallic and non-metallic dopants [36]. The surface properties of the CDs will be affected when the heteroatoms are doped into the CDs, which can positively influence the optical and biomedical properties of these materials [37]. When CDs with optical properties are irradiated with light, the molecules in the ground state will be excited, which, when passing through the excited singlet state, produces an excited triplet state. This excited triplet state can easily interact with the O2 molecules present in the biological systems under consideration by donating electrons or energy to them, thus producing reactive oxygen species [38]. This lays the principle for various applications like photodynamic therapy for cancer treatment. When the CDs are doped with metals and nonmetals, the gap between the singlet and triplet excited states decreases, which increases the ease of movement of electrons, thus enhancing the biomedical applications [39]. Doping with metals improves the chemical and physical properties of CDs like electronic bandgap, fluorescent lifetime, and QY [40–42]. However, metal doping can render CDs more toxic. Thus non-metal doping is preferred because of the simpler synthesis procedures and their nontoxicity [43]. Different non-metal heteroatoms, such as S, Si, N, and B doped CDs, capable of temperature sensing, metal ion detection, and cellular imaging, have broadened the applications of CDs in the areas of bioimaging and multi-target detection [34,44]. Doping with nitrogen enhances the photoluminescence QY, sulphur increases the photocatalytic activity, cerium improves wound healing capacity, and magnesium augments photosynthetic activity [35,45–47]. Compared to nonmetals, doping with metal ions, especially transition metal ions, is easier because of the electronic structures containing vacant d orbitals [34]. However, nitrogen, is the most preferred element for doping of CDs due to the similarities in electronic structure and size with carbon, and are used for various applications in the field of biomedicine [25].

Biomedical applications using CDs has evinced the interest of scientists for the past few years. CDs can be used for loading drugs efficiently and can function in drug delivery applications. Detection of one of the most widespread diseases of this century, cancer, is done using CDs. Photoluminescence, biocompatibility, low toxicity, etc., are some of the properties of CDs that make them useful in various biomedical applications [48–51]. J. Wang et al. have discussed the physicochemical properties and bioimaging-biosensing applications of CDs [52]. In another review, the structure, properties, bioimaging, cancer therapy, and optoelectronics applications of CDs have been discussed in detail [53]. S. Perumal et al. have explained the eco-friendly hydrothermal synthesis of CDs and their biomedical applications [54]. Different precursors, synthesis methods, characterization techniques, sensing mechanisms, and ion sensing of CDs were discussed by A.H. da Silva Junior et al. [55], and M. Kurian et al. reviewed the green synthesis methods of CDs [56]. J. Jia et al. have elaborated the various synthesis methods and their applications of room temperature phosphorescence in CDs [57], while C. He et al. mentioned the synthetic methods, photoluminescence mechanism, and applications of CDs [58]. L. Ansari et al. reviewed the synthesis methods, properties, and applications of doped CDs [59], and P. Bag et al. reviewed eco-friendly synthesis of CDs and applications in biomedicine and sensing [60]. The structure, fluorescence properties, synthesis, and biomedical applications of CDs were discussed by K. Ghosal et al. [61], B. Wang et al. discussed the applications of CDs in nanomedicine [62], and L. M. T. Phan et al. explained the bioimaging applications of fluorescent CDs [63]. L. M. T. Phan also discussed the fluorescent CD’s biomedical applications based on imaging [64]. A. Doring et al. reviewed the synthesis methods, various properties, and biomedical applications of chiral CDs [65].

Biosensing, bioimaging, cancer therapy, optoelectronics, biomedicine, and catalysis are the major applications of CDs and have already been discussed in many reviews. Table 1 gives the list of recent review articles that discuss the biomedical applications of composites using CDs. A detailed analysis of the literature shows that reviews on biomedical applications of metal and non-metal doped CDs have not been reported widely. As the usage of these composites in the biomedical field will efficiently enhance the healthcare systems prevailing in our society, a detailed study will enrich our knowledge in this field. This review focuses on the study of biomedical applications, synthesis methods, and properties of both metal and non-metal doped CDs, which are discussed in detail ( Fig. 1). This review will be of help to researchers who are onto biomedical applications using various CDs composites.

By selectively regulating the conditions suitable for reactions and the type of metal atoms, it is possible to influence the physicochemical properties of single metal-doped CDs, allowing for the exploration of even more cutting-edge uses for these materials [66]. Because of their robust antioxidant, antibacterial, photothermal/photodynamic, and antifungal properties, metal-doped and hybrid CDs have demonstrated intriguing biological applications [8]. The doping of CDs with metal atoms will provide enhanced optical properties and stability to them. Fluorescent properties will be higher in metal-doped CDs than CDs. Metal atom doping can improve the stability and QY of CDs and enhance the catalytic activity by modifying the surface chemistry of CDs. In a study by X. Wu et al., the Fe ions were incorporated into the CDs to synthesize Fe doped CDs, exhibiting improved chemodynamic and photoluminescence properties [33]. The enhanced catalytic activity reported in this study is due to the core-shell synergistic effect of the synthesized composite, in which the light absorbing chromophores and catalytic sites are blended in a manner that provide optimal results. This shows that, along with the surface modifications of CDs, the catalytic activity can also be enhanced through core-shell synergistic interactions.

Cerium doped CDs (Ce-CDs) with size 2–4 nm having good antioxidant activity and wound healing properties, were prepared hydrothermally using (CeNO₃)₃.6 H₂O and citric acid as precursors by M. Zhang et al. [67]. J. Guo et al. synthesized copper doped CDs (Cu-CDs) with enhanced optical activity and bright blue fluorescence by hydrothermal method using precursors L-Triptophan and copper chloride, which was used for bioimaging and biosensing applications [34]. Cu-CDs having good photocatalytic efficiency can be prepared from wood treated with chromium, copper, and arsenic as a precursor by hydrothermal method and found use in bioimaging [68]. Cu-CDs were prepared by carbonization from polyacrylic acid and Cu(NO3)2, and they had good photoinduced cytotoxicity and singlet oxygen QY which can be used for bioimaging [69]. Further, Cu-CDs with good peroxidase ability was prepared by pyrolysis using Cu(NO3)2·3 H2O and citric acid as precursors and used for glucose-sensing applications [70]. Silver doped CDs (Ag-CDs) having good antibacterial properties were prepared by one-step and two-step processes using polyethyleneimine (PEI), citric acid (CA), and AgNO₃ as precursors ( Fig. 2a). Those synthesized by the two-step process showed larger particle size, longer wavelength of emission, more silver content, and thus exhibited a higher antibacterial activity than the CDs prepared by the one-step process [71].

Gadolinium doped CDs (Gd-CDs) with size 2.45 ± 0.91 nm were synthesized by hydrothermal method using gadolinium nitrate hexahydrate and 3-aminothiophenol. They have a high fluorescence and can be used for bioimaging purposes [66]. Gd-CDs having good photoluminescence can be prepared by hydrothermal method using gadopentetic acid and glycine at 180 °C, which can be used for MRI assisted radiotherapy [72]. Gd-CDs with red bright fluorescence could be synthesized by solvothermal method using urea and citric acid and can be used for pH sensing [73]. X. He et al. synthesized Gd-CDs with high gene transfection efficiency using Gd(III) complex/salt, citric acid, and cationic polymers by hydrothermal method [74]. Cu-CDs was synthesized from cocoamidopropyl betaine as the precursor (Fig. 2b) [75]. Selenium doped CDs (Se-CDs) with excellent antioxidant properties were prepared from 1-selenocysteine by hydrothermal method [50]. Zinc doped CDs (Zn-CDs) have been prepared by hydrothermal method using sodium citrate and zinc chloride as precursors [76]. Nickel doped CDs (Ni-CDs) were prepared by electrolytic method using Ni dispersed porous carbon and polytetrafluoroethylene coated on carbon cloth [15].

Enhanced biocompatibility can be achieved through the incorporation of metals into CDs. However, it is noteworthy that an excessive concentration of metal doping can elevate the toxicity of the composite, thereby limiting the viability of these systems in the realm of biomedical applications. Consequently, there is a preference for the synthesis of metal-doped CDs with minimal metal content, but demonstrating high efficacy in diverse biomedical applications [77]. The metal doped CDs prepared are used for various biomedical applications ( Table 2) and are discussed in detail later in the article.

Different non-metal atoms have been used for doping CDs, nitrogen being the most common one due to its great resemblance with carbon in electronic structure and size [78–81]. Nitrogen doping can be done using precursors containing nitrogen during synthesis or by post-synthetic functionalization methodologies [25]. N-CDs exhibit good QY and high fluorescence properties and can be used for biomedical applications such as biosensing and bioimaging [82]. They can be synthesized in diverse ways, such as hydrothermal, calcination, pyrolysis, and carbonization methods. Of these, the hydrothermal method is the most widely used method.

Hydrothermally N-CDs can be prepared using different precursors. In one method, urea and humic acid were treated for 8 h at 180 °C [83], while in another, Lignin was hydrolyzed with mineral acids like nitric acid at 140–240 °C for 8–24 h to obtain N-CDs having size 3–6 nm and QY 54.6% ( Fig. 3a) [84]. L-tryptophan and chlorohexidine acetate treated at 200 °C for 12 h also yielded N-CDs having a size of 4.0 nm (Fig. 3b) [85].Y. Meng et al. hydrothermally synthesized orange fluorescent N-CDs having an average size of 3.1 nm using neutral red (NR) and glutamine (Gln) which were treated at 180 °C for 4 h [86].

A. Paul et al. prepared the same using jackfruit peel (JKP) and tamarind peels (TP) as precursors, treated at 180 °C for 12 h to get N-CDs showing blue fluorescence with QY and sizes of 13.04%, 6.4 nm for jackfruit peel and 6.13%, 6.13 nm for tamarind peel respectively [25]. The successful formation of CDs can be understood from the XRD pattern ( Fig. 4a). X. Zheng et al. prepared N-CDs from Panax notoginseng (Pn), a Chinese medicine treated with 1,2-ethanediamine (EDA) for 12 h at 180 °C. The particle size of the prepared Pn N-CDs is in the range of 10 nm (Fig. 4b) [4]. A.I. Kasouni et al. used citric acid and urea, at 200 °C for 3 h to produce N-CDs [87]. Using chitosan as a precursor, L. Sun et al. prepared N-CDs showing blue fluorescence, and average size of 2.8 nm, with a high QY of 35%. The HR-TEM images confirm the formation of N-CDs by the presence of lattice spacing of 0.213 nm (Fig. 4c-d) [88]. Citric acid and diethylenetriamine were treated for 6 h at 200 °C to get N-CDs having an average size of 5 nm [89].

Y. Newman Monday et al. synthesized N-CDs of 2 nm from palm kernel shells [13], while N. Nandi et al. synthesized it with an average size of 3.8 ± 0.9 nm and QY 22.7% using L-aspartic acid and 3,6-diaminoacridine hydrochloride [90]. Q. Liu et al. prepared N-CDs having size 3.32 nm and QY 20% by hydrothermal method with o-phenylenediamine and formamide as precursors [91] and A. Paul et al. prepared N-CDs with size 6.2 nm from water hyacinth, which was treated with deionized water [92]. R. Atchudan et al. synthesized the same using dwarf banana peel, a biowaste, as precursor [82]. The N-CDs synthesized by different methods and precursors have varied sizes, QY, and properties and show diverse biomedical applications, including bioimaging.

Besides hydrothermal method, calcination, pyrolysis, and solvothermal are the other methods used for the synthesis of N-CDs. N-CDs of size 1–4.5 nm can be prepared by calcination with QY 31.4% using citric acid and histidine in 10:1 ratio [43]. Pyrolysis method can be used, where sodium alginate and melatonin were taken as precursors to prepare N-CDs having an average size of 2.8 nm with QY 50.2% [93]. Using the same method N-CDs can be prepared using low molecular weight chitosan (LMWC) and silk fibroin (SF) blends which were treated for 3 h at 230 °C [16]. Also, N-CDs with an average size 2 nm was prepared by carbonization method using Zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF-8) filled with glucose (G@ZIF-8) ( Fig. 5a) [94]. F. Yang et al. synthesized N-CDs with size 10 nm by solid carbonization method without solvent using O-phenylenediamine and Al(NO3)3·9 H2O as precursors [95]. N-CDs having size 4.34 ± 1.13 nm and QY 11.4% could be prepared by solvothermal method with m-phenylenediamine and 1,2,3-propanetricarboxylic acid as precursors, which were treated for 8 h at 180 °C [96]. Y. Newman Monday et al. used palm kernel shell as precursor [13], while S. Yi et al. used p-aminoazobenzene as a precursor to prepare N-CD [97].

Even though different methods are used for the synthesis of N-CDs, the most widely used and effective method is the hydrothermal synthesis for its cost effectiveness, simplicity, and efficiency. It is a popular, eco-friendly, and inexpensive method for the synthesis of CDs that can produce monodispersed CDs with a high QY. Most of the works discussed here are based on hydrothermal method.

Sulfur is another environment-friendly and abundant element used for doping CDs. Doping sulfur results in stronger photoluminescence emissions and will enhance the photocatalytic activity [35]. Sulfur doped CDs (S-CDs) have been synthesized hydrothermally by treating aqueous turmeric solution, which contains ammonium persulfate [98], and in another method, it was prepared by treating sodium citrate solution and sodium thiosulfate for 6 h [99]. S-CDs have been prepared by one-step microwave treatment through condensation and further carbonization using thiourea and salicylic acid as precursors [35]. Silicon doped CDs (Si-CDs) with a size of 4.8 nm have been prepared by hydrothermal method using ethyl orthosilicate and o-phenylenediamine as precursors [100]. The reported non-metal doped CDs are listed in Table 3.

Non-metal codoped CDs are also reported and used for various biomedical applications. Nitrogen and sulfur are the most common elements for codoping CDs. The doping of these elements causes a reduction in the energy gap, an increase in QY, and a luminescence red shift [30]. The synthetic route of nitrogen and sulfur co doped CDs is shown in Fig. 5b. J.C. Jin et al. prepared nitrogen and sulfur codoped CDs (NS-CDs) by chemical cutting of polythiophene by nitric acid [2]. It can be synthesized using glutathione and p- phenylenediamine as precursors through a microwave-assisted method [30] and also using citric acid, water, and amino acids as precursors [101]. Another method for synthesizing NS-CDs is microwave-assisted solvothermal method using glutathione and sodium alginate in formamide [102]. Hydrothermally it can be synthesized by treating 3-amino-4-hydroxybenzenesulfonic acid (3-AHBSA) with deionized water for 10 h at 200 °C [103]. Also, NS-CDs with great fluorescence stability and water solubility can be prepared by hydrothermal carbonization method from 3-aminothiophenol and can be used for the detection of azithromycin in tablets and cells [104]. W. Dong et al. synthesized NS-CDs with good fluorescence efficiency by hydrothermal method using 3-amino thiophenol as a precursor for 12 h at 180 °C and can be used for biosensing applications [105]. C. Shi et al. prepared NS-CDs from renewable fibers of fungus by hydrothermal and biosynthesis methods. These CDs have good stability and water stability with a QY of 28.11% and can be used for biosensing applications [106].

Nitrogen and fluorine codoped CDs (NF-CDs) were prepared by solvothermal method at 180 °C for 12 h using 3,4-difluorophenylhydrazine [107]. L. Jiang et al. synthesized NF- co doped CDs for applications such as in vivo imaging and photodynamic therapy [12,108]. Nitrogen and iodine codoped CDs (NI-CDs) having size 2.2–4.6 nm and QY of 37% were prepared by hydrothermal method using iohexol as a precursor at 180 °c for 8 h [109]. Fluorine and chlorine doped CDs (F,Cl-CDs) with good antioxidant ability could be synthesized using sodium chloride, sodium fluoride, citric acid, and urea by hydrothermal method for 12 h at 200 °C [110]. Nitrogen, sulfur, and phosphorous codoped CDs (NSP-CDs) were prepared hydrothermally at 180 °C for 5 h using green pepper as a precursor [111]. Polyethylenimine (PEI) and cysteine were treated solvothermally to get NSP-CDs [112]. NSP-CDs with low toxicity and good photostability can be prepared hydrothermally from Saccharomyces cerevisiae, a waste microorganism, at 240 °C for 8 h and can be used for different biomedical applications such as cell labelling, biosensing, and diagnosis of disease [113]. Hydrothermally, boron, nitrogen, and sulfur codoped CDs (BNS-CDs) were prepared using 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid and 2,5-diaminobenzene sulphonic as precursors, which were treated for 8 h at 200 °C [24]. BNS-CDs can also be prepared hydrothermally, treating glucose, boric acid, urea, and sodium persulfate for 6 h at 200 °C [114].

Various reports are available where metal and non-metal have been codoped with CDs. Copper and nitrogen codoped CDs (CuN-CDs) were prepared by the solvothermal method using o-phenylenediamine and copper acetate monohydrate [115]. Gold and nitrogen codoped CDs(Au/N-CDs) were prepared using citric acid and ethylenediamine by hydrothermal method for 5 h at 200 °C [116]. B. Tian et al. hydrothermally synthesized nickel and nitrogen codoped CDs (Ni,N-CDs) with size 4.6 nm using ethylene diamine tetra acetic acid (EDTA), ethylenediamine (EDA) and hexahydrate nickel chloride as precursors at 180 °C for 6 h [117]. Cobalt and nitrogen codoped CDs (CoN-CDs) with size 2 nm and QY 4.46% were prepared using Co(NO₃)₂⋅6 H₂O and EDTA by calcination method [118]. Magnesium and nitrogen codoped CDs (MgN-CDs) were prepared by microwave synthesis using tamarind, water, and arginine as precursors [119]. P. Das et al. synthesized zinc and nitrogen codoped CDs (ZnN-CDs) having great luminescence by hydrothermal method using tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane and citric acid for 4 h at 200 °C and can be used as biosensor [120].

The doping of CDs with metals and non-metals enhances the properties of CDs, and, consequently, its efficiency towards various applications. These metal and non-metal doped CDs are widely used in applications, especially in the biomedical field. The list of co doped CDs reported, along with their applications, are listed in Table 4.

CDs are strong candidates for extensive biomedical applications such as bioimaging, biosensing, drug delivery, wound healing, pH sensing, cancer therapy, fluorescent probes, and gene delivery due to their superior features and physiochemical properties [3,34,83,85]. CDs could be modified with certain functional groups easily, and some of these applications are discussed in the following sections [85].

Wound healing is an important and intricate biological process and a significant healthcare concern on a global scale. In addition to being difficult to treat, wounds that don't heal properly could become chronic wounds that are in a state of pathologic inflammation and could make recovery a long and painful process, besides being a drain on the healthcare system. This can be overcome by shortening the healing time of wounds. Better therapies have resulted from research into wound healing mechanisms and the variables that affect their efficacy. Nanoparticles, especially CDs are effective candidates in this area [87,121].

The Ce-CDs obtained by the hydrothermal treatment of (CeNO₃)₃.6 H₂O and citric acid are good for cutaneous wound healing and can be applied either by spraying or wiping. The introduction of cerium will enhance the antioxidant capacity and hence the wound healing ability. In order to verify this, skin wound models made in Sprague-Dawley rats were treated with phosphate buffered saline powder (PBS), CDs, Ce(NO3)3⋅6 H2O, and Ce-CDs. It is found that Ce-CDs are superior to the other three groups in terms of speeding up tissue regeneration and wound healing ( Fig. 6a) [67]. N-CDs synthesized using citric acid as a precursor were found to be a good candidate for the wound healing process. In the skin of Wistar rats, two identical wounds were made, one as a test wound and the other as a control. A dosage of aqueous base cream, which was hydrophilic, and a dose N-CDs in the form of cream at different concentrations were applied, respectively, to the control and test wounds, and it was found that the wounds treated with CDs healed four days faster than control wounds (Fig. 6b) [87]. The NI-CDs prepared from iohexol have good antibacterial activity and thus could enhance wound healing capacity with the help of extremely low concentrations of H₂O₂. In order to study the wound healing ability of these CDs, a 10 mm diameter wound was made on the back of Sprague-Dawley rats, inoculated with 10⁷ colony-forming unit (CFU), treated with phosphate buffer solution (PBS), H₂O₂ (0.07 mM), NI-CDs (0.21 mg·mL−1) and H₂O₂/NI-CDs and is was found that of all the studied groups, the H₂O₂/NI-CDs group had the fastest rate of wound healing (Fig. 6c) [109].

Cu-CDs synthesized from cocoamidopropyl betaine were shown to have a wound healing efficiency of 96% within 14 days (Fig. 6d). This was studied by treating the wounds made in the skin of mice with phosphate buffer solution (PBS)+Laser, copper CDs (Cu-CDs), CDs-C35 (cocoamidopropyl betaine) + Laser, and Cu-CDs-C35 +Laser [75]. NSP-CDs prepared from waste onion peel powder are good probes for wound healing. Albino Wistar rats were subjected to full-thickness wounds and were divided as control and test, and PBS containing 1.5 mg/mL of NSP-CD was applied to the test on alternate days. The wounds treated with CDs showed 80% higher healing [122].

Nitrogen doped carbon dots-genipin covalent conjugate (N-CDs-GP), which contain N-CDs could be used for wound healing application. This was studied by taking mice as models, and it was found that wounds treated with N-CDs-GP healed faster than the control ( Fig. 7a) [85]. Poly-lactic acid (PLA)/ hyaluronic acid (HA)/ chitosan/ copper CDs (Cu-CDs)/ rosmarinic acid nanocomposite, which contains Cu-CDs have great wound healing ability. To a group of mice, wounds were made and studied with the positive control, PLA scaffold, PLA scaffold containing HA/chitosan/rosmarinic acid, and PLA scaffold containing HA/chitosan/rosmarinic acid/Cu-CDs 1% and the results compared (Fig. 7b) [123].

Bioimaging technology is crucial for both clinical practices and research and is developing rapidly. It is used to observe and investigate biological reactions from cellular or subcellular organisms up to small animals. CDs are good non-toxic fluorescent dyes and have a lot of potential for bioimaging since they can be used to visualize organisms, both in vivo and in vitro, and it is among the first uses of CDs that may be found. QY of CDs could affect bioimaging, and it can be improved by doping CDs with nitrogen [4,124]. The remarkable optical qualities possessed by CDs render them highly promising candidates for applications in bioimaging and biosensing. CDs exhibit optical absorption in the UV-Visible region. The distinct absorption bands, from 230 to 280 nm and 300–370 nm, can be attributed to π–π * and n–π * transitions, respectively. Notably, the wavelength at which CDs emit light surpasses their corresponding absorption wavelength. This phenomenon signifies that the emitted energy is of a lower magnitude than the absorbed energy. Upon exposure to an exciting wavelength of approximately 370 nm, CDs show a prominent emission peak around 430 nm, highlighting their exceptional fluorescence characteristics [42,125–127].

Sun et al. reported the first use of CDs for bioimaging in 2006 when they used CDs for labelling E Coli cells [128]. CDs have a number of benefits over conventional quantum dots and commercial organic dyes, including brilliant fluorescence and great biocompatibility, which make them the first choice for applications in bioimaging [129–131]. Monitoring the distribution of cells in vivo and identifying disorders, including cancer, ischemia diseases, and inflammatory diseases, need the use of bioimaging [42,132]. Through preclinical and clinical investigations, bioimaging has helped to develop cell-based therapeutics against a variety of diseases. Bioimaging applications will eventually cut the cost and time needed for the development of cell-based therapies [133,134].

The application of CDs as biosensors provide good accuracy for diagnostic and detection techniques. Because of good optical and photoluminescence properties, CDs are used for fluorescence-based DNA biosensors to detect pathogens and fluorescence imaging [13]. CD-based sensors work by different mechanisms such as charge transfer, ion binding reaction, and internal filtering effect [89]. Ce-CDs prepared from (CeNO₃)₃.6 H₂O and citric acid can be used in the treatment and visualization of integrated nanoplatforms in oxidative–related diseases [67]. Tetracyclines (TC) could be determined directly by Cu-CDs synthesized from L-tryptophan and copper chloride. Three types of TCs, oxytetracyclines (OTC), carbon tetrachloride (CTC), and TC, could quench the fluorescence in them and have little effect on some interfering particles. As the concentration of OTC increases, the fluorescence intensity of Cu-CDs decreases gradually. Thus, to detect TCs, Cu-CDs can be used as a fluorescence sensor because they have high selectivity and great sensitivity. Cu-CDs can enter into the nucleus and cytoplasm of the cell, and can be used for nucleus-targeted cell imaging [34]. N-CDs obtained from citric acid and histidine shows excellent results in the visual detection of dopamine(DA) and because of their excellent optical properties, can be used in the multicolor cellular imaging of MDA-MB 468 human breast cancer cells as a staining probe [43].

The N-CDs/Cu²⁺ complex could be used for the selective and sensitive detection of coenzyme A (CoA) in biofluids through its interaction with Cu²⁺ ion. Here, 1% human serum samples with different concentrations of CoA, 50, 100, and 200 nM were treated with N-CDs/Cu²⁺ and measured. CoA from the serum sample could be recovered with 96.4–106.4%, and this shows that it is a good fluorescence biosensor [93]. N-CDs synthesized from industrial waste lignin were found to be good for brain cell imaging applications. To evaluate this, N27 rat dopaminergic neural cells were incubated with these N-CDs, and toxicity, uptake, and luminescence performance were tested. It is found that with an increase in the concentration of N-CDs, the cell uptake rapidly rises with a maximum of 12.5% concentration. Also, it has low cell toxicity and good cell permeability, and thus it is good to use in bioimaging applications [12]. NS-CDs synthesized from polythiophene are used in cellular imaging. Fluorescent agents having low wavelengths are good in bioimaging because of their low light scattering and deep light penetration properties in biosamples. The λmax value of these CDs is around 600 nm and is suitable to conduct bioimaging tests. Also, based on the different metabolic activities of cells, these CDs can distinguish between normal and cancer cells. Thus NS-CDs are promising materials in cellular imaging applications. It is studied by treating gastric epithelial cell lines (GES-1) and Human epithelial carcinoma (HeLa) cells with NS-CDs, and red fluorescence was shown by both cells where HeLa cells were more intense than GES-1 cells ( Fig. 8a) [2]. NSP CDs produced from biowaste green pepper seeds can be used for Fe3 + fluorescence sensing. Further, it can easily enter the cytoplasm of cells, making it is a good fluorescent biological probe for imaging applications. While incubating HeLa cells with NPS-CD, it exhibits a blue fluorescence [111]. Ag-CDs synthesized from PEI, CA, and AgNO₃ have multicolor imaging capacity and can be used for bioimaging [71]. N-CDs-GP containing N-CDs can interact with gram-positive bacteria, which can be used to distinguish between gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria (Fig. 8b) [85].

N-CDs synthesized from chitosan are good multicolor fluorescent probes for bacterial imaging. E. coli and B. subtilis cells could be detected by this in blue, green, and red wavelengths with fluorescence (Fig. 8c) [88]. Tetracycline(TET), a therapeutic drug that is used for anti-inflammatory treatment in animal husbandry whose overconsumption causes risks to human health, can be easily detected by aminated central radial silica spheres (CRSSs-NH2 @N-CDs) biosensors which contain N-CDs prepared from diethylenetriamine. CRSSs-NH2 @N-CDs are dispersed in Britton-Robison (B-R) buffer solution of pH = 7.0. A small amount of this was pipetted, and TET solution with different concentrations was added and checked with a fluorescence spectrometer. It was found that TET has a quenching effect on CRSSs-NH2 @N-CDs as its fluorescence signal decreases gradually with the increase in the concentration of TET. Thus CRSSs-NH2 @N-CDs can be used as a good fluorescence sensor for TET detection [89].

N-CDs synthesized from m-phenylenediamine, and 1,2,3-propanetricarboxylic acid were used for bioimaging application of hypochlorite (ClOˉ) in cell nucleus and zebra fishes. After incubating with N-CDs for 4 h, Human epithelial carcinoma (Hela), MCF-7, and H1299 cells showed very high fluorescence, which is concentrated more in the nucleus (Fig. 8d). Also, while adding ClOˉ to these cells, the fluorescence intensity of the nucleus decreases with the increase in the concentration of ClOˉ when estimated in-vivo with zebrafish. It showed fluorescence while incubating with N-CDs, and it vanished when incubating with 750 μM of ClOˉ ( Fig. 9a). From these experiments, it could be understood that these N-CDs are a good tool for bioimaging applications. Also H. Wu et al. have suggested that it has promising applications in research on cancer treatment and targeted drug release applications [96]. S-CDs prepared using sodium citrate and sodium thiosulfate could be used as biosensors as they can sensitize Fe3 +, which is necessary for metabolic processes in living beings. Fe3 + gets absorbed on the surface of S-CDs and then coordinates with the phenolic ̶ OH group of S-CDs. Because of this, the excited electron of S-CDs gets transferred to the unfilled orbital of Fe3 + and thus gives rise to fluorescence quenching. The intensity of fluorescence decreases as Fe3 + is added to the S-CDs solution, indicating that it can be used for Fe3 + detection [99]. In a recent study, F- doped CDs were synthesized for dynamic imaging of the stress granules in cells [135].

S-CDs synthesized from salicylic acid and thiourea can be used for both in vitro and in vivo bioimaging, Leukemic monocyte-macrophage RAW264.7 mouse cells were incubated by S-CDs for in vitro bioimaging, and these cells exhibited bright luminescence under 405 nm excitation, which implies that these S-CDs can internalize into cells without causing any morphological change to the cells. For in vivo imaging, nude mice were injected with S-CDs and PBS. The mice with S-CDs showed good PL emission than the ones treated with PBS [35]. NS-CDs prepared from glutathione and p-phenylenediamine are used for bioimaging. Fe3 + can be detected at the cellular level by these CDs. To know this, two different types of cancer cells, Hela, and HL-60, and two types of normal cells, MDCK and COS-7, were incubated with NS-CDs. It is found that these CDs do not affect the viability of cells. To study Fe3 + detection capacity, Hela cells were incubated with NS-CDs for 6 h, and they exhibited bright fluorescence, which on treatment with Fe3 +, showed no fluorescence because it was quenched by Fe3 + [30]. NS-CDs prepared using sodium alginate and glutathione is good for bioimaging which is explained by treating HeLa cells with NS-CDs, and it exhibited bright red and blue fluorescence [102]. NS-CDs and NS-CD/Cysteine complex prepared using 3-amino-4-hydroxybenzenesulfonic acid (3-AHBSA) could be used for bioimaging which on treatment with HCT116 cells, and exposure to 10 μM cysteine increases the fluorescence [103].

Panax notoginseng (Pn) derived N-CDs are excellent for bioimaging applications, and it is used for imaging microorganisms, protozoa, plants, and animal species. E. coli, B. cereus, and S. cerevisiae bacteria were treated with N-CDs, and fluorescence was obtained in three different colors, namely, blue, green, and red. Protozoa named P. caudatum incubated with N-CDs exhibited fluorescence in three colors. For plant imaging, the onion’s inner epidermal cells showed three different color fluorescence. Also, leaves of N. physaloides were treated with these CDs and showed fluorescence. To study the imaging of animal systems, mice cells were treated with N-CDs and exhibited good fluorescence with variation in intensity with time (Fig. 9b) [4]. Dwarf banana peel-derived N-CDs were also used for bioimaging application. Clone 9 hepatocytes cells were treated with N-CDs, and they showed three different colors (red, green, and blue) fluorescence with different wavelengths [82]. N-CDs prepared from formamide and o-phenylenediamine were treated with Human laryngeal epidermoid carcinoma (HEp-2) cells, and the cells exhibited fluorescence with a bright yellow color which was quenched by adding Fe3 +, proving its utility for bioimaging applications [91]. N-CDs synthesized using L-aspartic acid and 3,6-diaminoacridine were used for cell imaging which was treated with HeLa cells, and this shows a blue fluorescence. It could also be used for sensing vitamin B12 and bilirubin [90]. Glutathione in human serum samples can be detected using N-CDs prepared from O-phenylenediamine and aluminum nitrate nonahydrate [95]. Gd-CDs were synthesized using Gadolinium nitrate hexahydrate, and 3-aminothiophenol, and is used for cell imaging application and morin detection [66]. CoN-CDs prepared from Co(NO₃)₂⋅6 H₂O and EDTA can be used for in vivo and in vitro H₂O₂ imaging and is evaluated by incubating CDs with HeLa cells and mice, where both exhibited a strong blue fluorescence [118]. Si-CDs prepared from o-phenylenediamine, and ethyl orthosilicate can also be used for bioimaging purposes [100]. Thus, by these experiments, it is found that the as-prepared CDs are good for bioimaging and biosensing applications.

For aqueous solutions, pH is one of the major properties as it can affect different physical and chemical changes. Thus, pH determination in various fields, such as biological, medical, agricultural, industrial, and environmental fields, is of great importance. Even though there are many methods for pH detection, CDs are more favorable as they have good biocompatibility and water solubility. Nitrogen, nitrogen and sulfur, nitrogen and phosphorous, nitrogen and fluorine, nitrogen and boron, and nitrogen and europium doped CDs are good for pH sensing [24]. It is very important to monitor pH because it plays a vital role in different chemical and biological systems. As the pH increases, the FL intensity of Cu-CDs prepared from L-tryptophan and copper chloride decreases significantly with an excitation at 369 nm [6]. The BNS-CDs synthesized from 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid and 2,5-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid have a great linear relationship with pH in the range of 1.6–7 and can apply in intracellular pH determination. To study this, human normal liver cell line (LO2) cells were incubated with BNS-CDs, and good fluorescence was exhibited ( Fig. 10a) [24]. The N-CDs prepared from neutral red, and glutamine can be used for cell imaging, intracellular pH variation, and ClOˉ sensing as they have a pH-sensitive response in the range of 4.0–9.0 and in the range of 5.6–7.4. Here, PC-12 cells were incubated with N-CDs, and it was found that these cells showed bright orange fluorescence at pH 4, and the fluorescence intensity decreased gradually as pH increased from 7 to 9. Also, while adding ClOˉ, fluorescence intensity decreased. Thus from these observations, we could understand that these N-CDs are an ideal agent for pH fluctuation and ClOˉ monitoring in living cells (Fig. 10b) [86].

Target specificity is the most important factor in developing a delivery system for an anticancer medication. The primary drawbacks of chemotherapy include the poor absorption of medications and adverse effects arising from killing healthy cells [16,136]. The functional groups on the CD's surface allow for covalent conjugation of the various drug-targeting moieties. To target the carrier to the affected cells, while sparing the healthy cells, nano-carriers would be conjugated with tumor-targeting moieties [16,137]. As opposed to dramatically reducing the fluorescence intensity of the CDs, covalent attachment of the ligands appear to promote the CDs’ accumulation in cancer cells more than in healthy cells. The fluorescent characteristics of conjugated CDs made it possible to analyze how the targeted systems were internalized by the cells [138,139]. When loaded with a drug or singlet oxygen, CDs with tunable surface groups have the potential for selectively aggregating in tumors or cancer cells and can either kill the cancer cells or prevent tumor growth. This makes them extremely useful for drug delivery and phototherapy [131]. CDs were able to traverse the blood-brain barrier in addition to having a strong capacity for selective targeting. Importantly, direct conjugation of different CDs increased the drug loading capacity of CDs without adversely affecting blood-brain barrier crossing, allowing for the treatment of Alzheimer's disease and brain cancer through drug delivery to the brain [140–142].

The N-CDs synthesized from jackfruit and tamarind peel have good anticancerous activity. Dalton’s Lymphoma Ascites (DLA) tumor cells of mice were treated with different concentrations of N-CDs and observed that these CDs could kill cancer cells. The dead cells appeared blue in color. Also, it was noticed that N-CDs prepared from jackfruit peel have more anticancerous activity than those from tamarind peel, and in both cases the activity increases with concentration of CDs [25]. In cancer diagnosis, analysis of cytochrome c (Cyt c) has an important role. The NF-CDs prepared from 3,4-difluorophenylhydrazine could be used as a probe for Cyt c, as it shows fluorescence while treated with a human serum sample containing Cyt c [107]. CDs confined in N-doped carbon (CDs@NC) can be used to detect gastric cancer in the early stage by colorimetrically analyzing the saliva , as the D-Proline (D-Pro) and D-Alanine (D-Ala) concentration in saliva will be greater for the gastric cancer patients than healthy people in the early stages. Saliva samples and D-amino acid oxidase (DAAO) were incubated, CDs@NC and Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) solutions were added, and then UV–vis spectra were recorded. A blue color is formed for the samples containing D-Pro and D-Ala as they produce H₂O₂, which oxidizes colorless TMB to blue oxTMB. This color change can be observed with the naked eye, and it is a quicker and simpler method ( Fig. 11) [94]. Ni,N-CDs prepared from EDTA, EDA, and hexahydrate nickel chloride can be used for photothermal therapy (PTT) of cancer treatment. HeLa cells were treated with these CDs, and on continuous laser irradiation of 1064 nm, they exhibited strong red fluorescence indicating it could kill the cancer cells [117]. The N-CDs prepared using low molecular weight chitosan (LMWC) and silk-fibroin (SF) blends are good for cancer treatment. Biotin, which can target cancer cells, was conjugated with these CDs, and showed more cytotoxicity in MCF-7 (Human breast cancer cell lines) cancer cells than in HEK-296 (Human embryonic kidney cell lines) normal cells. MCF-7 cancer cells exhibited more blue bright fluorescence than normal cells when treated with CDs [16]. Gd-doped CDs synthesized from gadopentetic acid is a good probe for MRI diagnosis of tumor [72]. An emerging trend in cancer therapy is photodynamic therapy. In a recent study, Mn- doped CDs were used for targeting the mitochondria of the cells for treating tumors [39].

The other biomedical applications utilizing CDs are antioxidant studies, cell labelling, antibacterial studies, photodynamic antimicrobial chemotherapy, and DNA/RNA monitoring. NPS-CDs synthesized from PEI and cysteine can be used as a good antioxidant ( Fig. 12a) [112]. S-CDs prepared from turmeric and ammonium persulfate exhibited good antioxidant activity, which can be studied using 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl radical (DPPH) and 2,2′ -azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) methods. It shows 60% and 80% radical scavenging activity with DPPH and ABTS methods, respectively [98]. Au/N- CDs synthesized from citric acid ethylenediamine are used as antioxidants. Hydroxy radicals generated by the Fenton reaction between Fe²⁺ and H₂O₂ were treated with 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid (HTA), which then showed good fluorescence. On addition of Au/N-CDs to this, the intensity of fluorescence gets reduced, which shows the CDs’ hydroxyl scavenging ability. It was also studied with 2,2′-azino--bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) as Au/N- CDs can quench its radicals [116]. Se-CDs prepared from 1-selenocysteine is a good antioxidant for kidney injury treatment, and it is further validated by treatment with animal and murine models [50].

CuN-CDs synthesized from o-phenylenediamine, and copper acetate monohydrate were found to be suitable for labelling human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs). To study this, HUVECs were treated with CuN-CDs and found that they show red fluorescence in the presence of CDs (Fig. 12b) [115]. N-CDs prepared using p-aminoazobenzene is good for cell labelling and were studied by treating HeLa cells with CDs and found that they exhibited a red fluorescence and good cell penetrating power, independent of energy and temperature [97]. MgN-CDs prepared from tamarind and arginine can be used for biolabelling [119]. The cause for the antibacterial activity of CDs is their small size, as it can interfere with the metabolism of cells by easily penetrating the cells of bacteria [98]. Ag-CDs synthesized using PEI, CA, and AgNO₃ have antibacterial properties for various microorganisms and thus could be used as good antibacterial agents (Fig. 12c) [71].

NI-CDs synthesized from iohexol show efficient antibacterial activity for S. aureus and E. coli bacteria. It will increase upto 100% when the irradiation period of visible light is increased to 60 min when exogenous H₂O₂ is present. Depending on the concentrations of NI-CD, H₂O₂, and exposure to visible light, NI-CDs with peroxidase activity can kill S. aureus and E. coli in the presence of H2O2 ( Fig. 13a) [109]. N-CDs-GP containing N-CDs are good antibacterial agents as they can cause harm to the bacterial cell walls, resulting in internal electrolyte leakage and, thereby, death of the cells. This occurs by the interaction between the negatively charged lipid membrane with the bacterial cell walls through hydrophobic and electrostatic interactions [85].

S-CDs synthesized from turmeric and ammonium persulfate show good antimicrobial functions. The antibacterial activity of S-CDs can be studied using the zone of inhibition method. It could be seen that S-CDs exhibited antimicrobial activity against E. coli and L. monocytogens bacteria with the zone of inhibition being 18 ± 2.3 mm and 14 ± 1.5 mm for L. monocytogens and E. coli, respectively. The reason behind the good antibacterial activity of S-CDs is the presence of sulfonic acids and sulfonates as functional groups. These groups can destroy bacteria by binding with its ̶ SH group to form reactive oxygen species (ROS), which denature lipids and proteins, thereby destroying the bacteria (Fig. 13b) [98]. Red fluorescent N-CDs prepared from p-aminoazobenzene can cleave RNA chains. An enhancement in fluorescence can be seen on adding RNA to N-CD solution. On gradual addition of RNA, the fluorescence initially increases and then decreases with time. These CDs could be used as nano agents for intracellular nucleolus imaging [97]. Photodynamic therapy has received a lot of attention in the biomedical field. The irradiation of light can activate the CDs and exhibit properties that can be utilized in various applications in the biomedical field. The incorporation of photosensitizers into the CDs facilitates the creation of photodynamic antibacterial CDs, capable of effectively impeding bacterial activity. The benefits of these type of materials are that the drug resistant strains can be eliminated easily and provide a wide range of antibacterial activity [143].

Photodynamic therapy (PDT) has garnered substantial interest within the realm of biomedicine. By subjecting carbon dots (CDs) to light irradiation, their activation leads to the manifestation of properties amenable to diverse biomedical applications. Integrating photosensitizers into CDs facilitates the creation of photodynamic antibacterial CDs, capable of effectively impeding bacterial activity. Clearly superior, these materials offer the ability to easily eliminate drug-resistant bacterial strains while providing an expansive scope of antibacterial efficacy.

Carbon dots (CDs) are nanomaterials with zero-dimensional properties, displaying strong fluorescence, and are devoid of metals. Due to their exceptional inherent benefits, they have drawn more attention in many biomedical applications. The introduction of heteroatom doping further enhances the physical and chemical features of CDs. This comprehensive review investigates the impact of doping on CDs and explores their significant biomedical applications. Here we have discussed metal-doped, non-metal-doped, and co-doped CDs, outlining their synthesis methods and biomedical uses. Notably, nitrogen emerges as the predominant choice for CDs' doping, while the hydrothermal approach stands out as the preferred synthesis method. In the biomedical area, doped CDs find use in biosensing and bioimaging applications. Heteroatom-doped carbon nanomaterials enhance their intrinsic characteristics, broadening their scope across domains such as electrochemistry, photocatalysis, sensing, and bioimaging. Doped CDs, with sizes below 10 nm and having impurities added during production, enhance their optical, electrical, and chemical capabilities. Doped CDs exist in single and multiple configurations based on the extent of atomic impurities incorporated. Since nitrogen's atomic radius is comparable with carbon, nitrogen is the most commonly used heteroatom for the creation of doped CDs. Due to their good photophysical properties, biocompatibility, and bio-applicability, N-CDs have indicated excellent potential in various biomedical fields, such as bioimaging, biolabelling, and cancer therapy. Non-metal-doped CDs, on the other hand, augment CDs' optical, electronic, and photophysical attributes. Metal atom doping induces alterations in CDs' band structure, enhancing charge transfer and electron-hole recombination. Both metal and non-metal doped CDs have a wide range of applications, especially in the biomedical field.

Since the initial discovery of CDs in 2004, scientists have made significant improvements in synthesis methods to tailor their properties for various applications. Identifying optimal preparation approaches for CDs that offer enhanced properties and expanded applications is paramount, especially in biomedicine. In contrast to other carbon nanomaterials, CDs exhibit distinctive advantages, including exceptional fluorescence performance, favourable biocompatibility, simple synthesis, and ease of functionalization. However, certain challenges persist, such as improving quantum yield, extending fluorescence lifetime, and enhancing optical stability, particularly in the context of doped CDs. Efforts aimed at surmounting these challenges are imperative to pave the way for the synthesis of efficient doped CDs. A crucial strategy involves developing diverse synthetic schemes to enable doping with a wide range of elements possessing varied physical and chemical properties. Overcoming these hurdles holds the potential to position doped CDs as viable nanomaterials for biomedical applications in the forthcoming years.

Anumol Mathew: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Arun Varghese: Writing – review & editing, Writing, Figures, Editing. Sunaja Devi K. R.: Data curation, Investigation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Dephan Pinheiro: Writing – review & editing.
